text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic lavage, using blunt dissection to take down adhesions of the small bowel from the abdominal wall and the medial aspect of the sigmoid colon.
The surgeon uses three 5mm laparoscopic ports to break up loculations and mobilize the small bowel out of the pelvis, aiming to visualize the area of perforation.

The surgeon reaches the pelvis anteriorly over the sigmoid colon and inspects the area laterally, ensuring no additional loculations need to be broken up or drained. The left pelvic side wall is visible.

The surgeon performs lavage in the pelvic region, ensuring thorough irrigation without antibiotics. The small bowel is separated from the medial aspect of the sigmoid, which is attached to the right pelvic sidewall. The surgeon inspects for any remaining abscesses, noting the potential adherence to the bladder.

The surgeon inspects the surgical field, ensuring no pus is present and confirming that all dominant areas have been drained. They clean up fibrinous exudate and take a sample for culture. The small bowel is examined for any inadvertent injuries from blunt separation, and the omentum is repositioned.

The surgeon places a drain in the pelvis following the laparoscopic lavage procedure. The video shows the isolation of the superior hemorrhoidal artery, located distal to the takeoff of the left colic artery.

The surgeon takes the mesenteric vasculature in the current location to aid in reach, selectively mobilizing the splenic flexure. The superior hemorrhoidal artery is taken just distal to the left colic artery. Medial dissection is performed, revealing the ureter, which is highlighted using ICG for easier identification.

The surgeon addresses the area in the right pelvis where the appendix is adhered to an abscess cavity. The appendix is carefully dissected to avoid injury, but ultimately removed using a silk tie instead of a staple load.

The surgeon bluntly separates tissue on the right pelvic sidewall, encountering pus indicative of a residual phlegmon and a possible sinus tract. The area is adjacent to the iliac vessels and the right ureter. The surgeon uses suction in the left hand to clear blood and fluid, while dissecting with scissors in the right hand.

The surgeon attempts to break through the residual rind of the abscess cavity to peel the rectosigmoid away. Encountering difficulty, the surgeon proceeds with a dissection along the right rectal area, similar to a total mesorectal excision (TME), moving anterior to the abscess cavity and leaving it on the sidewall. This maneuver successfully elevates the rectosigmoid, freeing it for further lateral dissection.

The surgeon identifies a natural white line in the mid to distal sigmoid colon, noting the absence of diverticula in the proximal sigmoid and descending colon. The medial dissection continues towards the splenic flexure, with ports placed in a diagonal orientation towards the patient's right.

The surgeon isolates the rectosigmoid for transection, opting to divide at the upper rectum to avoid anastomosis near the phlegmon. The rectal mesentery is taken, and the rectum is mobilized below the peritoneal reflection for additional length, with careful dissection under the seminal vesicles and prostate to preserve nerves.
The surgeon takes the proximal mesentery, following the initial marked margin. The area of demarcation is visible a few centimeters proximal to the planned margin, prompting a new mark to be made.

And then same on the rectum. This was a case just because the rectum was a little bit inflamed from the prior or the abscess, the chronic abscess that was there. And my transection was a little bit lower than I usually do. I elected not to do a transrectal extraction in this case, although there probably would have been nothing wrong with doing it. I just had this feeling that I would inadvertently tear the rectum, and then my anastomosis would go like to a more lower, low anterior resection. I didn't think that was necessary. So I chose to do a fan and seal extraction of the specimen after division.

The surgeon places the sigmoid colon into the right fossa and prepares for a hand-sewn anastomosis. The back staple lines are aligned and sewn together. The rectum is mobilized below the peritoneal reflection, visible in the pelvis. A proctotomy and colotomy are made with scissors. Indocyanine green (ICG) is used to assess the mucosa, which appears healthy. Suturing begins in the center, extending outwards to the far corner, then from the center towards the near corner.

The surgeon observes a wide diameter of both the rectum and sigmoid, facilitating a wide open anastomosis. The transition to an anterior canal is performed, with suturing from the near corner outward using a right hand forehand technique, extending across to the other suture.

The surgeon ties two sutures together for security, ensuring closure on one side. Using a 3-0 V-Loc suture, the surgeon invaginates the anterior suture line over the top of the back row. The technique involves a two-layer anastomosis, contrasting with single-layer methods.

The surgeon performs a leak test using rigid proctoscopy, introducing fluid posteriorly to prevent false alarms from air bubbles. The scope reveals a wide open anastomosis with no bleeding or leaks visible.
